rankings.title: The Real Reason to End the Death Penalty
summary_bullets: 4% of death row inmates are innocent.
Wrongful murder convictions are very common.
Police and prosecutors often ignore exculpatory evidence.
Many exonerated after years on death row.
Capital punishment kills innocent people in practice.

rankings.title: How to Write Usefully
summary_bullets: Useful writing is bold but true.
Four components: correctness, importance, novelty, strength.
Morris technique: only publish what you're sure of.
Write simply and revise extensively.
Novelty often provokes resistance.

rankings.title: How People Get Rich Now
summary_bullets: Inheritance declined from 60% to 27%.
Most new fortunes come from startups.
Fund managers emerged as new rich.
73 new fortunes in 2020.
56 from founders/early employees.

rankings.title: How to Earn a Billion Dollars
summary_bullets: Billionaires often come from startups.
Exponential growth makes billion possible.
Growth rate and market size determine wealth.
Make something users love to get growth.
Young founders should build for themselves.

rankings.title: Putting Ideas into Words
summary_bullets: Writing reveals incomplete knowledge.
First words are usually wrong.
Rewriting is necessary for precision.
Half of essay ideas emerge during writing.
Reading as stranger tests correctness.

rankings.title: Is There Such a Thing as Good Taste?
summary_bullets: Argues good taste exists via reductio.
Denying good taste denies good art.
Art's effectiveness depends on human commonalities.
Analogy: art like vaccine effectiveness.
Taste is partial order, not total.

rankings.title: Haters
summary_bullets: Haters are obsessive, uncritical critics.
They are fanboys with reversed sign.
Haters regard targets as frauds.
Ignore haters like obsessive fans.
Haters are frustrated, unsuccessful people.

rankings.title: How to Think for Yourself
summary_bullets: Some work requires novel ideas.
Scientists need correct and novel ideas.
Investors profit from non-obvious insights.
Most work only needs correctness.
People misjudge their independence.

rankings.title: Billionaires Build
summary_bullets: YC looks for founders who understand users.
Startups must make something people want.
Billionaire success not from exploitation.
YC partners guess market potential.
Interview focuses on new, uncertain needs.

rankings.title: Being a Noob
summary_bullets: Old people don’t have everything figured out.
Feeling like a noob signals novelty, not stupidity.
Aversion to noob feeling is an evolutionary leftover.
More local noob means less global ignorance.
Embrace noob feeling for growth.

rankings.title: Write Simply
summary_bullets: Simple writing boosts reader engagement.
Low friction helps readers finish.
Fancy writing can hide empty ideas.
Simple writing lasts longer.
Author writes simply out of personal distaste.

rankings.title: Writes and Write-Nots
summary_bullets: Writing is fundamentally difficult.
AI removes pressure to write.
Future split: writes vs. write-nots.
Writing equals thinking.
Only those who choose will be smart.

rankings.title: Founder Mode
summary_bullets: Brian Chesky talk at YC event.
Conventional scaling advice damaged Airbnb.
Founders feel gaslit by managers.
Two modes: founder vs manager.
No books on founder mode yet.

rankings.title: Early Work
summary_bullets: Fear of making lame work holds people back.
Early ambitious projects often seem unimpressive.
Silicon Valley treats strange ideas as challenges.
Dismissing new ideas can be a social custom.
Optimism about early work can be learned.

rankings.title: When To Do What You Love
summary_bullets: Following passion is not simple yes/no.
Trade-off between passion and money.
Exception when passion and demand align.
Strange tastes can be profitable.
Some have intellectual interest in making money.

rankings.title: Donate Unrestricted
summary_bullets: Restricted donations limit how money is used.
Nonprofits usually know best where funds needed.
Restricted donations often suboptimal.
Exceptions: umbrella orgs, domain experts.
Donors have motives beyond doing good.

other.title: The Airbnbs
summary_bullets: Airbnb founders were exceptionally earnest and energetic.
They persisted despite near-failure and maxed-out credit cards.
Focus on NYC and professional photos sparked growth.
Ramen profitability ($4k/month) was key milestone.
They took notes and implemented ideas immediately.

other.title: Earnestness
summary_bullets: Earnestness is highest compliment for founders.
Earnest means right motives and maximum effort.
Earnestness alone insufficient; need capability too.
Combined with formidable, founders nearly unstoppable.
Motives as vectors: direction and magnitude.

other.title: Weird Languages
summary_bullets: Most programming is glue code.
Weird languages reveal new programming techniques.
Lisp macros enable writing problem-specific languages.
Learning weird languages expands thinking.

other.title: What to Do
summary_bullets: Help people and take care of world.
Make good new things.
Best proof of good thinking.
Newness essential for impressiveness.
Two kinds of "should": duty vs potential.

other.title: Beyond Smart
summary_bullets: Einstein's special quality was new ideas.
Intelligence and achievement are not identical.
Many smart people achieve little.
Childhood overvalues intelligence.

other.title: How to Convert Between Wealth and Income Tax
summary_bullets: Wealth tax 1% equals income tax 20%.
Conversion divides by rate of return.
Assumes 5% risk-free return.
Politicians ignore this conversion.

other.title: Coronavirus and Credibility
summary_bullets: TV journalists made false coronavirus predictions.
Epidemic rapidly falsifies predictions.
Pandemic reveals who lacks credibility.
Warren Buffett quote about tide going out.
Remember who was wrong.

other.title: The Best Essay
summary_bullets: Best essay must be surprising.
Topic must be important.
Often describes scientific discovery.
Darwin’s 1844 essay is example.
Science enters like an elephant.

other.title: Good Writing
summary_bullets: Two kinds of good writing: sound and ideas.
Claims good sound correlates with correctness.
Argues from personal writing experience.
No trade-off between sound and truth.
Fixing bad sentences helps ideas.

other.title: A Project of One's Own
summary_bullets: Self-directed projects feel like skating vs walking.
Great work often comes from personal projects.
Kids' projects can lead to adult careers.
Society separates play from work unnecessarily.

other.title: Crazy New Ideas
summary_bullets: Don't dismiss crazy ideas from experts.
Reasonable experts know it sounds crazy.
History shows big ideas start crazy.
Most implausible ideas are bad, but not from experts.
Expert's crazy idea suggests they know something.

other.title: What I've Learned from Users
summary_bullets: Startups share common problems.
YC learns from many startups.
Early-stage funding yields data.
Individual advice still needed.
Problems recur across different startups.

other.title: The Right Kind of Stubborn
summary_bullets: Persistence and obstinacy may be same behavior.
Labels depend on eventual success.
Distinction matters only if behaviors differ.
Essay asks to tease apart real differences.

other.title: The Brand Age
summary_bullets: Swiss watch industry hit by three 1970s disasters.
Japanese watch quality surpassed Swiss by 1968.
Bretton Woods collapse made Swiss watches 2.7x costlier.

other.title: How to Get New Ideas
summary_bullets: Notice anomalies: strange, missing, broken.
Look at frontiers of knowledge.
Knowledge grows fractally with gaps.
Gaps yield new ideas.

other.title: Alien Truth
summary_bullets: Universal truths beyond math and physics.
Heuristic: truths shared with aliens.
Related to Erdős's God's book.
Philosophy should seek alien truths.

other.title: The Origins of Wokeness
summary_bullets: Prigs exist in every society.
Wokeness is modern priggishness about social justice.
Originated in 1980s as second wave of political correctness.
Defined as aggressively performative focus on social justice.
Problem is performativeness, not social justice itself.

other.title: How to Work Hard
summary_bullets: Hard work essential for great work.
School standards were low.
Famous adults practiced years.
Three ingredients: ability, practice, effort.
Effort makes hard work look easy.

other.title: The Reddits
summary_bullets: Paul Graham met Reddit founders before YC.
Original idea was mobile food ordering.
YC funded them to build link-sharing site.
Reddit grew despite later management issues.
Steve Huffman returned to revive Reddit.

other.title: An NFT That Saves Lives
summary_bullets: Noora Health launches NFT to save lives.
Saves 9 babies per 1000 live births.
Cost per life saved: $1,235.
NFT price determines number of lives saved.
Reserve price $2.5M for 2000 lives.

other.title: The Shape of the Essay Field
summary_bullets: Essays must tell readers something new.
Three reasons for ignorance yield three essay types.
One type: unimportant but interesting details.
Example: essay about a specific car model.

other.title: Superlinear Returns
summary_bullets: Returns for performance are superlinear, not linear.
Teachers' "you get out what you put in" is misleading.
Superlinear returns appear in business, fame, power, knowledge.
The rich get richer across many domains.

other.title: Heresy
summary_bullets: Heresy concept is being reborn.
Newton avoided three unforgivable sins.
Heresy historically meant religious dissent.
Modern society revives heresy accusations.
Essay explores this surprising trend.

other.title: Fierce Nerds
summary_bullets: Nerds can be fierce, not just quiet.
Fierce nerds are extremely competitive.
They tend to be overconfident, especially young.
Intelligence is common among fierce nerds.
Confidence can become self-fulfilling.

other.title: The Need to Read
summary_bullets: Sci-fi imagined brain-loading tapes replacing reading.
Such replacement is unlikely anytime soon.
Reading teaches writing, not just content.
Even perfect knowledge upload would be insufficient.

other.title: Modeling a Wealth Tax
summary_bullets: Wealth tax reduces stock over time.
1% tax takes 45% of stock in 60 years.
Formula: keep 99% each year, compound.
Table shows tax rates vs. government take.

other.title: The Four Quadrants of Conformism
summary_bullets: Classifies people by conformism and aggressiveness.
Four quadrants: aggressive conventional, passive conventional, passive independent, aggressive independent.
Claims types depend on personality, not society.
Uses Cartesian coordinate system.

other.title: How to Do Great Work
summary_bullets: Intersection of great-work techniques has definite shape
First step: choose work with aptitude, interest, scope
Ambitious people are too conservative about scope
Find something you have aptitude and interest in

other.title: What I Worked On
summary_bullets: Worked on writing and programming before college.
Wrote awful short stories with no plot.
First programs on IBM 1401 using Fortran.
Learned programs could fail to terminate.
Microcomputers changed everything.

other.title: How to Start Google
summary_bullets: Startups let you avoid having a job.
Startups range from barber shops to Google.
Y Combinator funded over 4000 startups.
Startup success is unpredictable.
Aim for same chance as Google.

other.title: What You (Want to)* Want
summary_bullets: Puzzled by free will vs. determinism since age 9.
Hoped to argue he wasn’t responsible for actions.
Lost hope but puzzle remained.
